calendar.matters:
CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Jul-31-2017 Off Calendar

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar May-19-2017 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On Aug-01-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File / Notice Of And Application For Leave To File Complaint In Intervention (Ccp 387 (A)); Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Apr-10-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. SHAWN MCCANN OF BD&J LLP, TEL. NO. 1-310-887-1818 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFFS;
ATTY. SAMMY OBAID, TEL. NO. 1-510-433-9100 APPEARING FOR THE INTERVENOR.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Proposed Intervenor State of California Department of Transportation's application to file a complaint in intervention is denied. The State has not shown that it has satisfied the requirements for either mandatory or permissive intervention. While the State claims an interest in the insurance policy of defendant Jlayi Deng, that is not an "interest relating to the property or transaction" within the meaning of that phrase as used in CCP 387(b), the mandatory intervention statute. Per California Physicians' Service v. Superior Court (1980) 102 Cal. App. 3d 91, there is no "property" for purposes of section 387(b) in a case such as this one involving only tort claims. Also, per California Physicians' Service, the "transaction" that is being litigated in this case is the collision between Mr. Deng's vehicle and Mr. De Leon's vehicle and the resulting injuries to Mr. Giron and Mr. De Leon, not the collision between Mr. Deng's vehicle and property owned by the State and the resulting damage to the State. So defined, the State has no interest in the "transaction which is the subject of" this action and thus is not entitled to intervene as of right per section 387(b). Because allowing the State to intervene in this case would enlarge the issues in the case to include the collision between Mr. Deng's vehicle and property owned by the State and the resulting damage to the State, the State is not entitled to permissive intervene either. Even if permissive intervention was available to the State, the court would exercise its discretion to deny tis motion because granting the motion would require a trial continuance, which is unfairly prejudicial to Mr. Giron and Mr. De Leon. Neither of the cases cited in the State's reply memorandum are apposite. As a third party unrelated to the plaintiff who allegedly suffered distinct harm from the harm suffered by the plaintiffs based on Mr. Deng's asserted negligence, the State has no right to intervene in this case, even though as a practical matter that may mean that the State would not be able to collect on any judgment it may obtain against Mr. Deng. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Laura A. Reding, CSR 711 =302/hk
